clinical_indications: Continuous escape of urine per vaginum (true incontinence) due to Vesicovaginal fistula (VVF).
Presence of Vesicovaginal fistula (VVF) caused by obstetrical factors (ischemic, traumatic) or gynecological factors (operative injury, traumatic, malignancy, radiation, infective).
Associated pruritus vulvae due to VVF.

preauth.label: Detailed Clinical notes with history, indications, symptoms, signs, examination findings and advice for admission

ppd_preauth.q: Are all vitals, detailed history, symptoms, signs, physical examination including local examination, indication for procedure, planned line of treatment, and advice for admission documented in the detailed clinical notes?
Did clinical presentation and/or imaging confirm the diagnosis?

cpd_claim.q: Are detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details provided?
Are detailed procedure / operative notes available?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advice at the time of discharge submitted?
Was the clinical presentation indicative of surgery?

common_queries: Clinical presentation, physical examination, and/or imaging was not indicative of surgery.
Cystoscopy/Cystourethroscopy report not provided at pre-authorization.
Complete Urine Examination (CUE) report not provided at pre-authorization.
Planned line of treatment is not clearly documented at pre-authorization.
Detailed procedure/operative notes are missing or incomplete at claim submission.

clinical_key_pointers: In Vesicovaginal fistula (VVF), there is a communication between the bladder and the vagina and the urine escapes into the vagina causing true incontinence. This is the commonest type of genitourinary fistula.
surgical_options: Transvaginal repair (preferred method of repair in most cases)
Latzko technique (for repair of uncomplicated post hysterectomy VVF)
Interposition grafts (can be used for tissue bulk and as a source of neovascularity)
Abdominal approach (especially O'Conor technique) (should be reserved for situations in which access to the fistula is limited)
